title: Teaching
text:
My teaching focuses on helping students use geospatial tools to solve real problems, communicate clearly, and build professional confidence. I teach GIS, cartography, uncrewed aerial systems, remote sensing, spatial analysis, and professional-development topics through applied projects and realistic workflows.
Rather than treating GIS as a collection of disconnected tools, I try to help students understand the full problem-solving process: defining the question, identifying useful data, evaluating limitations, choosing appropriate methods, producing clear deliverables, and explaining results to an audience.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Areas

Geographic Information Systems: Applied GIS workflows, spatial analysis, data management, and problem solving.
Cartography and Geovisualization: Map design, visual communication, audience awareness, and effective geospatial storytelling.
Uncrewed Aerial Systems: FAA Part 107 concepts, flight planning, data collection, photogrammetry, safety, and applied UAV workflows.
Remote Sensing: Image interpretation, sensor characteristics, data products, and applications.
Professional Preparation: Portfolio development, conference preparation, project communication, career pathways, and professional expectations.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Approach
I design courses and labs around realistic scenarios. Students are often asked to work through problems similar to those they might encounter in professional practice: identifying what data are needed, determining whether the data are appropriate, choosing tools and methods, producing deliverables, and communicating results clearly.
This approach helps students see GIS as a process rather than a button-clicking exercise. The goal is not simply to complete a tutorial, but to understand how geospatial analysis supports decision-making.

title: TNGIC Education and Outreach Leadership
text:
The TNGIC Education and Outreach Leadership project reflects my professional service through the Tennessee Geographic Information Council, with a focus on GIS education, student engagement, professional development, and outreach across Tennessee’s geospatial community.
This work connects students, educators, practitioners, public agencies, private-sector professionals, and community partners through conferences, workshops, outreach activities, professional networking, and educational programming.

title: TNGIC Education and Outreach Leadership
text:
Service Role
My role has included leadership and committee service focused on education, outreach, student engagement, conference planning, professional development, and strengthening connections between higher education and the broader GIS community.

title: TNGIC Education and Outreach Leadership
text:
Service Goals
This work supports:

expanding opportunities for students to engage with the GIS profession,
connecting educators and practitioners across Tennessee,
supporting professional-development opportunities,
building stronger pathways between academic programs and geospatial careers,
encouraging student participation in conferences, presentations, competitions, and networking,
strengthening the visibility and value of GIS education and outreach.

title: TNGIC Education and Outreach Leadership
text:
Leadership Activities
Selected activities include:

education and outreach committee leadership,
student-focused programming and professional development,
conference planning and support,
outreach activities connecting GIS students with professionals,
support for K–12 and higher-education geospatial education,
collaboration with professional, academic, public-sector, and community partners.

title: TNGIC Education and Outreach Leadership
text:
Portfolio Significance
This service work reflects my broader commitment to building geospatial community, supporting students, strengthening professional networks, and helping people see GIS as both a technical field and a collaborative professional community.
It also connects directly to my work in teaching, mentoring, outreach, and program development.

title: TNGIC Education and Outreach Leadership

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
The Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products page highlights selected geospatial outputs developed as part of a historic cemetery mapping project. These products were designed to support interpretation, documentation, and communication of landscape features that may be difficult to identify from ground-level observation alone.
This work demonstrates how UAV imagery, LiDAR-derived elevation products, cartography, and spatial visualization can support community-engaged projects while also creating meaningful examples for geospatial teaching and student learning.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Geospatial Focus
This portfolio item emphasizes the technical and communication products developed through the project, including:

UAV imagery and orthomosaic products,
LiDAR-derived elevation and surface models,
hillshade and surface visualization products,
map layouts and interpretive cartography,
spatial documentation of visible and subtle landscape features,
visual materials for project communication.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Methods and Workflow
The workflow included field data collection, geospatial data processing, spatial interpretation, map production, and communication with project partners. Products were developed to help translate complex spatial data into useful visual information for interpretation, planning, and discussion.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Cartographic and Communication Goals
The geospatial products were designed to be more than technical outputs. They needed to communicate clearly with audiences who may not regularly work with UAV data, LiDAR, elevation models, or GIS software.
Important design considerations included:

making subtle landscape patterns visible,
choosing appropriate visualizations for elevation and surface features,
balancing technical accuracy with readability,
presenting information in ways that could support discussion with partners and public audiences,
acknowledging limitations in what the data can and cannot show.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Portfolio Significance
This work is a strong example of applied geospatial communication. It demonstrates how remote sensing, UAV mapping, LiDAR visualization, and cartography can be used together to support a real project with community value.
It also connects directly to my teaching philosophy: GIS products are not just software outputs; they are part of a larger process of asking questions, evaluating data, interpreting results, and communicating clearly.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Related Project
This geospatial portfolio item is connected to the broader Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project.

title: Workshops & Outreach
text:
My outreach work focuses on helping students, educators, professionals, public agencies, and community partners use geospatial tools more effectively. These activities often connect GIS, cartography, UAVs, remote sensing, and professional development with real-world needs beyond a traditional classroom setting.
Workshops and outreach events allow me to translate technical geospatial topics into practical learning experiences for different audiences, including undergraduate students, K–12 educators, working professionals, community organizations, and public-sector partners.

title: Workshops & Outreach
text:
Outreach Areas

FAA Part 107 and UAV Education: Workshops and learning materials that help participants understand airspace, regulations, weather, sectional charts, airport operations, and safe UAV operations.
GIS Professional Development: Training opportunities focused on practical GIS workflows, geospatial problem solving, map communication, and applied spatial analysis.
K–12 Educator Support: Professional development activities that help teachers connect geospatial tools, UAVs, maps, and spatial thinking to classroom learning.
Community and Public Outreach: Events and projects that make geospatial technology more accessible to community partners and public audiences.
Student Professional Development: Activities that help students prepare for conferences, internships, research opportunities, portfolios, and careers in GIS and geospatial technology.

title: Workshops & Outreach
text:
Approach
I try to design workshops around what participants actually need to do after the event. Rather than focusing only on software demonstrations, I emphasize practical workflows, decision points, common mistakes, and the reasoning behind technical steps.
This approach is especially important in geospatial outreach because participants often need to apply what they learn in complex, real-world settings. A useful workshop should help people understand not only which buttons to press, but also why the workflow matters, what limitations to watch for, and how to communicate results clearly.

title: About
text:
I am a GIS educator, outreach coordinator, and community-engaged geospatial practitioner at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville. My work focuses on helping students, educators, professionals, and community partners use geospatial tools to understand problems, communicate clearly, and make better decisions.
My professional work sits at the intersection of teaching, outreach, applied GIS, uncrewed aerial systems, cartography, and student mentoring. I develop applied learning experiences that connect technical skills with real-world questions, including GIS analysis, web mapping, UAV data collection, remote sensing, cartographic communication, and professional development.

title: About
text:
Professional Focus
My work includes:

Teaching undergraduate and professional-development courses in GIS, cartography, UAVs, remote sensing, and geospatial problem solving.
Developing workshops and outreach programs for students, educators, public agencies, and professional audiences.
Supporting community-engaged GIS and UAV projects with students, colleagues, and partner organizations.
Mentoring students as they prepare for conferences, research opportunities, internships, jobs, and graduate school.
Contributing to professional organizations, outreach initiatives, and geospatial education efforts across Tennessee.

title: About
text:
Approach
I try to design learning experiences around realistic problems rather than isolated tools. In GIS, the technical steps matter, but they are only part of the work. Students and professionals also need to understand the question being asked, the data needed to answer it, the limitations of the analysis, and the best way to communicate results to a real audience.
This portfolio highlights selected examples of that work, including teaching materials, workshops, community projects, geospatial products, mentoring activities, service roles, and professional recognition.

title: About
text:
Contact
Contact information and professional links will be added here.

title: Community GIS Projects
text:
My community-engaged GIS work focuses on using geospatial tools to support partner organizations, public-facing projects, applied research, and student learning. These projects often combine mapping, spatial analysis, UAV data collection, remote sensing, cartography, and communication with real-world questions and community needs.
Many of these projects also serve as learning opportunities for students. They provide a way to connect technical GIS skills with professional expectations, partner communication, project management, data limitations, and public-facing deliverables.

title: Community GIS Projects
text:
Project Areas

Historic Preservation and Cultural Landscapes: Mapping and documenting cemeteries, historic sites, and community landscapes.
Environmental and Conservation Mapping: Supporting conservation, land management, trail planning, and natural-resource questions.
Public Infrastructure and Facilities: Using GIS and UAV data to support inspection, documentation, planning, and communication.
Community Partner Support: Developing maps, analyses, and geospatial products for organizations with specific applied needs.
Student-Engaged Applied GIS: Creating opportunities for students to participate in meaningful geospatial projects beyond the classroom.

title: Community GIS Projects
text:
Approach
Community GIS projects require more than technical analysis. A successful project depends on understanding the partner’s question, identifying appropriate data, communicating limitations, and producing deliverables that are useful to the intended audience.
I try to approach these projects as collaborative problem-solving efforts. The goal is not simply to create a map or dataset, but to help partners better understand places, patterns, conditions, and decisions.

title: Geospatial Portfolio
text:
This page collects selected examples of maps, web applications, UAV products, cartographic outputs, spatial analyses, and geospatial communication materials. These examples represent work developed for teaching, outreach, community projects, professional service, and applied problem solving.
My geospatial work emphasizes clear communication, appropriate methods, useful deliverables, and audience-centered design. Whether the final product is a printed map, web application, UAV-derived product, report figure, or workshop example, the goal is to help people understand spatial information and use it effectively.

title: Geospatial Portfolio
text:
Portfolio Areas

Cartography and Map Design: Static maps, thematic maps, reference maps, and visual communication products.
Web GIS and Interactive Applications: Web maps, dashboards, Experience Builder applications, StoryMaps, and other interactive tools.
UAV and Remote Sensing Products: Orthomosaics, surface models, inspection imagery, photogrammetry products, and remote-sensing outputs.
Spatial Analysis and GIS Workflows: Applied analyses developed for teaching, outreach, community partners, and student projects.
Geospatial Communication: Figures, reports, presentations, and visual materials that translate spatial information for specific audiences.

title: Geospatial Portfolio
text:
Approach
I think of geospatial products as communication tools, not just technical outputs. A good map or GIS product should be accurate, readable, purposeful, and appropriate for the audience using it.
This means paying attention to data quality, cartographic design, uncertainty, context, accessibility, and the decisions the product is meant to support.

title: Mentoring & Student Success
text:
My mentoring work focuses on helping students build confidence, develop professional skills, and identify meaningful pathways in GIS, geography, sustainability, remote sensing, UAVs, and related fields. This includes supporting students as they prepare for research opportunities, internships, conferences, graduate school, professional networking, and careers.
I see mentoring as part of geospatial education. Technical skills matter, but students also need help understanding how to talk about their work, connect with professional communities, evaluate opportunities, and imagine possible futures for themselves.

title: Mentoring & Student Success
text:
Mentoring Areas

Undergraduate Research: Supporting students as they develop project ideas, prepare posters and presentations, and share their work with academic and professional audiences.
Conference Preparation: Helping students prepare for events such as TNGIC, ASPRS, SEDAAG, EUReCA, ASUReS, and other professional or academic meetings.
Career Pathways: Helping students explore GIS, planning, environmental, UAV, public-sector, private-sector, nonprofit, and graduate-school options.
Professional Communication: Supporting students as they build resumes, portfolios, abstracts, presentations, posters, and professional introductions.
Community and Professional Connection: Encouraging students to participate in professional organizations, outreach events, applied projects, and networking opportunities.

title: Mentoring & Student Success
text:
Approach
I try to help students see themselves as emerging professionals, not just students completing assignments. This means helping them recognize the value of their work, explain their skills clearly, and connect classroom experiences to real opportunities.
Mentoring often involves translating between academic requirements, professional expectations, and individual student goals. The goal is to help students make informed choices and build confidence through experience.

title: Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project
text:
The Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project is a community-engaged geospatial project focused on supporting documentation, interpretation, and preservation of historic cemetery landscapes.
The project used geospatial technologies such as UAV imagery, lidar, mapping, and spatial analysis to help identify landscape features, document site conditions, and communicate information about cemetery spaces that can be difficult to interpret from ground-level observation alone.

title: Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project
text:
Project Role
My role included coordinating geospatial data collection, supporting UAV and lidar workflows, working with students and colleagues, developing project deliverables, and helping translate technical geospatial outputs into useful information for project partners and public audiences.

title: Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project
text:
Project Goals
This project was designed to support:

documentation of historic cemetery landscapes,
identification of visible and subtle landscape features,
development of geospatial products for interpretation and planning,
student participation in applied GIS and UAV workflows,
communication with community partners and stakeholders.

title: Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project
text:
Geospatial Methods and Products
Project activities included a combination of field data collection, UAV-based mapping, lidar-derived surface information, cartographic production, and spatial interpretation.
Selected geospatial products included:

UAV imagery and orthomosaic products,
lidar-derived elevation and surface information,
maps and visualizations for interpretation,
spatial documentation of landscape features,
project materials for communication with partners.

title: Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project
text:
Teaching and Student Engagement
This project provided students with an opportunity to see how GIS, UAVs, remote sensing, and cartography can support real community questions. Students were able to connect technical workflows with issues of historic preservation, landscape interpretation, data limitations, and public communication.
The project also helped demonstrate that applied GIS work is rarely just a single tool or software operation. It involves understanding the partner’s question, collecting appropriate data, evaluating limitations, producing useful deliverables, and communicating results clearly.

title: Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project
text:
Portfolio Significance
This project reflects the intersection of community-engaged GIS, UAV mapping, lidar, student learning, and public-facing geospatial communication. It is a strong example of using geospatial technology to support partners while also creating meaningful applied learning opportunities for students.

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
The Decoding Part 107 Workshop is a professional-development workshop designed to help participants understand the knowledge areas required for safe and legal small uncrewed aircraft systems operations.
The workshop focuses on FAA Part 107 concepts, aviation decision-making, airspace, airport operations, sectional charts, weather, loading and performance, radio communications, and practical preparation for the remote pilot certification exam.

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
Project Role
My role includes developing workshop materials, organizing instructional content, teaching key aviation and geospatial concepts, building practice resources, and adapting the workshop for different audiences, including educators, students, and geospatial professionals.

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
Teaching and Outreach Goals
This workshop is designed to help participants:

understand why FAA Part 107 matters for educational and professional UAV use,
interpret aviation charts and airspace information,
identify weather and operational risk factors,
connect UAV operations to real-world geospatial applications,
build confidence using aviation resources and terminology,
prepare for additional study toward the FAA Remote Pilot Certificate.

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
Selected Components

FAA Part 107 instructional materials
Sectional chart and airspace interpretation activities
Weather and airport operations examples
Practice quizzes and study resources
Educator-focused UAV use cases
Workshop materials adapted for K–12 and professional audiences

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
Portfolio Significance
This project reflects the intersection of my work in geospatial education, UAV outreach, professional development, and applied teaching. It also demonstrates how technical aviation material can be translated into practical learning experiences for audiences who may not have prior aviation backgrounds.

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
Related Links
Links to public materials will be added here.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
The Applied GIS Lab Design approach reflects my effort to teach GIS as a complete problem-solving process rather than a sequence of disconnected tools.
In many GIS courses, students learn individual software operations without always understanding how those operations fit into a larger workflow. My goal is to design labs that begin with realistic questions, require students to identify and prepare appropriate data, guide them through analysis and interpretation, and end with deliverables that resemble what they might produce for a client, supervisor, agency, or community partner.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Teaching Role
My role includes developing lab scenarios, writing step-by-step technical instructions, creating supporting videos and examples, designing deliverables, and helping students understand how individual GIS tools fit into a larger analytical process.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Teaching Goals
This approach is designed to help students:

understand GIS as a problem-solving process,
connect technical steps to real-world questions,
evaluate data needs and limitations,
practice complete workflows rather than isolated tools,
produce clear maps, figures, analyses, and written explanations,
build confidence for internships, jobs, research, and community projects.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Instructional Approach
I often frame GIS assignments as realistic scenarios. Students may be asked to support a planning question, analyze environmental conditions, prepare public-facing maps, evaluate spatial patterns, or develop products that communicate results to a specific audience.
The goal is not only to complete a tutorial. The goal is to understand what the workflow is doing, why each step matters, what assumptions are involved, and how the final product should be communicated.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Portfolio Significance
This teaching approach connects directly to my broader work in geospatial education, professional development, community-engaged GIS, and student mentoring. It reflects the idea that GIS students need both technical competence and the ability to reason through complex spatial problems.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Related Materials
Examples of selected labs, course materials, or public teaching resources will be added here after review.

title: Service & Leadership
text:
Service has been a central part of my professional career. I believe that geospatial education is strengthened through collaboration, professional engagement, and community involvement. My service activities focus on expanding opportunities for students, supporting the geospatial profession, strengthening educational programs, and connecting universities with practitioners, educators, and community partners.
These leadership roles have included departmental service, statewide professional organizations, conference planning, outreach initiatives, strategic planning, and collaborative projects that extend beyond the classroom.

title: Service & Leadership
text:
Areas of Service

Professional Organizations: Leadership and committee service supporting the geospatial profession and geospatial education.
Conference Planning: Organizing conferences, workshops, and educational programming that connect students, educators, professionals, and community partners.
Program Development: Creating new educational opportunities, outreach programs, workshops, and student experiences.
Department and University Service: Supporting curriculum development, student engagement, outreach, recruitment, and program growth.
Community Engagement: Building partnerships that connect university resources with public agencies, nonprofit organizations, educators, and local communities.

title: Service & Leadership
text:
Philosophy of Service
I view service as an extension of teaching. Bringing people together, creating opportunities, sharing resources, and helping organizations solve problems all contribute to strengthening the geospatial community.
Many of the most rewarding projects I have worked on have resulted from long-term relationships between universities, professional organizations, educators, students, and community partners.
